perturbation.original_text: I agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Confidentiality Agreement by me and Williston may be entitled to equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, in addition to all other remedies available to it at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: I agree that money damages may not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Confidentiality Agreement by me and Williston may be entitled to equitable relief, possibly including injunction and specific performance, in addition to other remedies at law or in equity it deems fit.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear entitlement to equitable relief. The modified text weakens this entitlement by introducing uncertainty ('may not be sufficient', 'possibly including', 'it deems fit'), making it harder to enforce specific performance or injunctions. This ambiguity contradicts the principle of specific performance in contract law, which requires clear and definite terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles Regarding Specific Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 states that specific performance will not be decreed if damages would be adequate to protect the injured party. By weakening the assurance that money damages are insufficient and making equitable relief discretionary, the modified text undermines Williston's ability to obtain specific performance, creating ambiguity about the enforceability of this crucial remedy. This introduces uncertainty that violates the intent of ensuring enforceability.
perturbation.location: Paragraph 3 (Likely - Based on Common Confidentiality Agreement Structure)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the availability of equitable relief, specifically specific performance and injunctions. The original text asserts that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy and that Williston 'may be entitled' to equitable relief. The changed text weakens this by stating that money damages 'may not be' sufficient and that Williston may be entitled to equitable relief, 'possibly including' injunction and specific performance. The scraped law snippet defines specific performance as a contractual remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract, rather than simply paying damages. By making equitable relief discretionary ('it deems fit') and uncertain ('may not be sufficient', 'possibly including'), the modified text undermines the enforceability of specific performance, creating a legal contradiction by introducing an ambiguity in a critical remedy.

perturbation.original_text: This Confidentiality Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Confidentiality Agreement shall be generally guided by the laws of the State of Delaware, considering relevant legal principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that Delaware law governs the agreement. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'generally guided by' and 'considering relevant legal principles,' which undermines the binding nature of Delaware law, making it unclear which laws definitively apply. This contradicts established principles of contract interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Interpretation - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows parties to choose the law that governs their agreement, and that choice is typically upheld if not against public policy. The modified text, by using 'generally guided' and 'considering,' weakens the choice of law provision, making it less certain that Delaware law will actually govern. This ambiguity can lead to disputes over which law applies and renders the contract less predictable and enforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. [Last reviewed in August of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS multi-jurisdictional law conflicts of law THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure legal practice/ethics wex articles wex definitions civil procedure constitutional law criminal law and procedure legal education and practice Resources menu of sources Federal Material U.S. Constitution Article VI - Supremacy Clause ; CRS Annotated text Article IV, Section 1 - Full Faith and Credit Clause ; CRS Annotated text Federal Statutes 28 U.S.C. §§1738-1739 - State Proceedings 18 U.S.C. §§ 848 , 896 , 927 , 2345 47 U.S.C. § 741- Satellite and Communications Law 19 U.S.C. § 2504 - Trade Agreements 45 U.S.C. § 1213 - Railroads Federal Judicial Decisions U.S. Supreme Court: Recent Conflicts Decisions liibulletin Oral Argument Previews U.S. Circuit Courts of Appeals: Recent Conflicts Decisions State Material State Judicial Decisions N.Y. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Choice of law - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Legal process to determine whose law applies Conflict of laws Preliminaries Characterisation Choice of law Conflict of laws in the United States Incidental question Judicial interpretation Renvoi Public policy doctrine Hague Conference Definitional elements Jurisdiction Procedure Forum non conveniens Lex causae Lex fori Forum shopping Lis alibi pendens Connecting factors Domicile Lex domicilii Habitual residence Nationality Lex patriae Lex loci arbitri Lex loci rei sitae Lex loci contractus Lex loci delicti commissi Lex loci actus Lex loci solutionis Lex loci protectionis Proper law Lex loci celebrationis Choice of law clause Dépeçage Forum selection clause Substantive legal areas Status Capacity Contract Tort Marriage Divorce Property Forced heirship Hague Trust Convention Enforcement Enforcement of foreign judgments Anti-suit injunctions Revenue rule v t e Choice of law is a procedural stage in the litigation of a case involving the conflict of laws when it is necessary to reconcile the differences between the laws of different legal jurisdictions , such as sovereign states , federated states (as in the US), or provinces . The outcome of this process is potentially to require the courts of one jurisdiction to apply the law of a different jurisdiction in lawsuits arising from, say, family law , tort , or contract . The law which is applied is sometimes referred to as the " proper law ." Dépeçage is an issue within choice of law. Sequence of events in conflict cases in Common Law jurisdictions [ edit ] Jurisdiction . The court selected by the plaintiff must decide both whether it has the jurisdiction to hear the case and, if it has, whether another forum is more suitable (the forum non conveniens issue relates to the problem of forum shopping ) for the disposition of the case. Naturally, a plaintiff with appropriate knowledge and finance will always commence proceedings in the court most likely to give a favourable outcome. This is called forum shopping and whether a court will accept such cases is always determined by the local law. Recognition of foreign judgments . Even where a conflict of laws exists, the court will recognize the validity of a foreign judgment in most cases. Under U.S. law, this authority is part of the Full Faith and Credit Clause of the U.S. Constitution. Under international law, this authority is part of the doctrine of comity . The court will invoke comity by its discretion and will usually look to two factors before using its discretionary powers: did the foreign court have jurisdiction, and were fair procedures used in adjudicating the case? Under English law, it is the doctrine of obligation . Within the European Union the Brussels Recast Regulation determines jurisdiction and recognition. Characterization . The court then allocates each aspect of the case as pleaded to its appropriate legal classification. Each such classification has its own choice of law rules but distinguishing between procedural and substantive rules requires care. The court may have adopted a rule of law which prevents it from applying any procedural law other than its own. This can include the court's own choice of law rules. A danger exists if the choice of law requires that a case be heard elsewhere due to the forum's lack of expertise in deciding an issue of foreign law. The court then applies the relevant choice of law rules. In a few cases, usually involving family law, an incidental question can arise which will complicate this process. The United States has adopted a law that almost universally eliminates incidental questions involving family law. The Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction and Enforcement Act (UCCJEA) requires states to apply the law of the "home state;" that is, the forum which originally determined custody and maintenance. A state court will only apply its own law when no parent retains a connection with the original jurisdic
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original, clear choice of Delaware law to a more ambiguous 'generally guided by', introducing uncertainty about which laws apply. This directly undermines the principles of contract interpretation and choice of law, as parties are allowed to choose the law that governs their agreement. The law snippets reinforce that choice of law is a procedural stage in litigation, and this modification makes the contract less predictable and enforceable. The modification creates a situation where it is unclear which jurisdiction's laws will be used, thus creating an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction

perturbation.original_text: I will not disclose Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: I will endeavor not to disclose Confidential Information to most third parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear and strict prohibition against disclosing confidential information. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'endeavor not to' (suggesting an effort rather than a guarantee) and 'most third parties' (leaving open the possibility of disclosure to some third parties). This creates a weaker, less enforceable obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Duty of Confidentiality
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Agency § 396 addresses the duty of an agent (in this context, the employee) not to use confidential information. The original provision aligns with this duty by creating a strict prohibition against disclosure. The modified text weakens this prohibition, creating a loophole that could be exploited, and undermining the clear duty of confidentiality that the law seeks to uphold. It makes the obligation vague.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by introducing ambiguity in the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text established a strict prohibition. The modified text weakens this, by using 'endeavor not to', suggesting an effort rather than a guarantee, and limiting the prohibition to 'most third parties'. This undermines the clear duty of confidentiality. The HeinOnline snippet, while not directly stating the law, highlights the importance of accessible and authoritative legal documents, indirectly supporting the need for clear and enforceable contract terms, which the modified text fails to provide.

perturbation.original_text: I acknowledge and agree that the covenants contained in this Confidentiality Agreement are necessary for the protection of Williston’s legitimate business interests and are reasonable in scope and restriction.
perturbation.changed_text: I acknowledge that the covenants in this Confidentiality Agreement may be seen as relevant for the business interests of Williston and are arguably reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: The original text is a clear acknowledgment that the covenants are necessary and reasonable. The modified text uses weaker language like 'may be seen as relevant' and 'arguably reasonable,' introducing doubt about the necessity and reasonableness of the covenants. This could undermine the enforceability of the agreement, particularly if the reasonableness of the scope and restriction is challenged in court. This creates ambiguity and impacts the enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Reasonableness of Restrictive Covenants
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts require that restrictive covenants, such as those in a confidentiality agreement, be reasonable in scope and duration to be enforceable. By weakening the acknowledgment of reasonableness, the modified text provides grounds for a challenge to the enforceability of the covenants under Delaware law, introducing ambiguity that contradicts the original, more affirmative statement. The use of words like 'may' and 'arguably' diminish the validity of the covenants.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: restrictive covenant | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restrictive covenant A restrictive covenant is a provision in a real property conveyance that limits the grantee’s use of the property. The beneficiaries of a restrictive covenant obtain rights from such covenants, and this may be the parties who agreed to the restrictive covenant or adjunct property owners who benefit from the restrictive covenant. For example, in Ezer v. Fuchsloch , a California Court of Appeals affirmed a lower court order requiring property owners to cut down certain trees on their property to the level of their single-story house pursuant to a restrictive covenant which provided that “no tree, shrub, or other landscaping should be planted that would at present or in the future obstruct the view [of the Pacific Ocean] from any other lot.” However, courts will not blindly uphold all restrictive covenants and may not enforce them if it would infringe on civil liberties or if the party seeking enforcement lacks standing . For example, in the famous 1948 U.S. Supreme Court Case, Shelby v. Kramer , the Court struck down the enforcement of a restrictive covenant which provided that “only Caucasians may hold title” because it violated the Equal Protection Clause . As another example, in Birt v. Ratka , the New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division extinguished a restrictive covenant prohibiting defendants from subdividing property that had right-of-way over landowner's property, holding that “[pursuant to N.Y. Real Property Code] [a] restrictive covenant shall not be enforced if, at the time enforceability of the restriction is brought into question, it appears that the restriction is of no actual and substantial benefit to the persons seeking its enforcement.” See also covenant not to compete . [Last reviewed in April of 2021 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex PROPERTY land use & zoning law property & real estate law wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original acknowledgment of the covenants' necessity and reasonableness by using phrases like 'may be seen as relevant' and 'arguably reasonable.' This introduces ambiguity regarding the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement. The original text clearly acknowledges the necessity and reasonableness which is a stronger position in court. The second scraped snippet discusses restrictive covenants and mentions that courts may not enforce them if they infringe on civil liberties or if the party seeking enforcement lacks standing. By weakening the language, the changed text gives the opposing party an opening to challenge the covenants, making it arguably contradictory as it undermines the enforceability that the original contract was trying to establish.
